question: A modeler draws a throwing link event named `Retry` at the root process level and a catching link event with the same name inside an embedded subprocess, intending to jump from the root into the subprocess. What is the result?

options.text: The link does not connect, because link events can only link sections of a process within the same scope.
The link works because matching link names connect any throw to any catch globally across scopes.
The link works only if both events also share a Camunda-specific `linkScope` attribute.
The catching link inside the subprocess is automatically promoted to the root scope so the link can connect.

a.text: Per the docs, link events can only be used to link sections of a process within the same scope, so a root-level throw cannot pair with a catch inside a subprocess.

b.text: Link names match only within the same scope; they are not a global pairing mechanism across scopes.

c.text: There is no such attribute; the same-scope restriction is not overridable.

d.text: BPMN does not promote events across scopes. The same-scope limitation applies to both link events and sequence flows.

explanation: Link events are scope-local shortcuts equivalent to a pair of intermediate none events joined by a sequence flow. They can only pair within the same scope — either both at root level or both inside the same subprocess — and cannot cross subprocess boundaries.
